title: 七面钟之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\144-七面钟之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Lady Cutt expresses her sorrow to Jimmy Sycamore regarding Sir Oswald's plan to give their home to a company, criticizing his low taste and his insatiable drive for wealth and status. She notes that Sir Oswald places significant trust in Mr. Bettman, a diligent worker at his factory. During their conversation, Jimmy recalls a pleasant weekend at the Chimney House but notes the sadness surrounding Gerry's death. Lady Cutt asks Jimmy if he is interested in Vera Daventry, whom she nicknames "the socks." Jimmy expresses a desire to see Vera again, and Lady Cutt invites him to visit their house the following weekend during her visit.

After Jimmy departs, Sir Oswald expresses his dislike for the boy, labeling him a troublemaker. Lady Cutt informs Oswald that she has already invited Mr. Cecil to visit the following weekend. Oswald objects, revealing that Mr. Bettman warned him to be cautious of Cecil because the two were former classmates. While Lady Cutt expresses regret over the misunderstanding, she continues her knitting with a subtle smile.

Elsewhere, Lord Kettlemere discusses golf with Eileen Bündel. Lord Kettlemere has taken up the game with enthusiasm but poor skill, frequently tearing up the turf, which McTavish attempts to repair. Bündel suggests that McTavish's frustration with the Cutlers is finally being met with "just desserts." Bunty remarks that golf keeps Lord Kettlemere out of trouble, while Lord Kettlemere claims to have achieved a hole-in-one. Bunty also observes that while Osborne Cutt's golf technique is stiff and accurate, he is unable to make short putts, causing him to lose games.
